symbols: arrows piercing the body — the instruments of Sebastian's martyrdom
bound arms raised above — willing submission to suffering, echoing Christ's posture on the cross
upward gaze — appeal to heaven and trust in divine deliverance
muscular athletic body — Sebastian's identity as a Roman soldier
putti at his feet — angelic comfort and divine recognition of the martyr's sacrifice
near-nudity — vulnerability and the stripping away of worldly protection
description: A Baroque painting depicting a young muscular man bound with his arms raised above his head, tied to an unseen post. His body is pierced by several arrows. He gazes upward with an expression mixing pain and spiritual ecstasy. He wears only a cloth around his waist. At his feet, a small putto or cherub looks up at him. Behind him, partially visible figures appear to be untying or attending to him. The background is very dark, with dramatic light falling on the pale, athletic body of the central figure.
theological_description: Saint Sebastian, a Roman Praetorian Guard secretly Christian, was condemned to death by arrows under Emperor Diocletian for his faith. His martyrdom, though post-biblical, embodies the scriptural theology of faithful witness unto death. Sebastian's bound, arrow-pierced body visually echoes the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53 and the willingness of the apostles to suffer for Christ's name (Acts 5:41). His upward gaze reflects Stephen's heavenly vision at his own martyrdom (Acts 7:55-56). The arrows, traditionally symbols of divine judgment in the Psalms (Psalm 38:2: 'Your arrows have sunk into me'), are here transformed into instruments of martyrdom willingly received. Sebastian's military identity — a soldier of the empire who became a soldier of Christ — embodies Paul's exhortation to 'put on the whole armor of God' (Ephesians 6:11) and to 'share in suffering as a good soldier of Christ Jesus' (2 Timothy 2:3). The tradition that Sebastian survived his first execution only to confront the emperor again and be beaten to death speaks to the theology of Revelation 2:10: 'Be faithful unto death, and I will give you the crown of life.'

scripture_refs.reason: Be faithful unto death and I will give you the crown of life — the call to martyrdom Sebastian embodies
Stephen's upward gaze at his martyrdom — the archetypal Christian martyr paralleled by Sebastian
Share in suffering as a good soldier of Christ — Sebastian as the literal soldier who became Christ's martyr
For your sake we are being killed all the day long — the ongoing pattern of Christian martyrdom
